unitTitle: Y8 Layers of computing systems (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Get in gear — what makes a computer a computer
lessons.objectives:
I can say that a computer is a device for running programs
I can explain that a program is a list of instructions that work on data
I can explain the difference between a general-purpose computer and a purpose-built machine
I can give examples of programs (software) I use
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: computer · program · software · instructions · data · general-purpose. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — old machines vs modern computers. Show the Analytical Engine image on the slide; pupils CHOOSE on the starter worksheet what a modern computer can do that an old calculating machine cannot. Think · pair · share.
   • Support: tick-box choice (run programs / one sum / nothing).  • Core: name a program used today.  • Challenge: name one way the old machine is the SAME as a modern computer (input → process → output).
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — a computer RUNS PROGRAMS. Teacher defines program = a list of instructions that work on data (I do). Class sorts everyday things into purpose-built device vs general-purpose computer (we do). Likely error: “a phone is just a phone” → fix-words “a phone runs MANY programs, so it is general-purpose.”
3. (20–35) You-do — the activity worksheet. Card-sort PROGRAM (software) vs DATA. Then fill the gap sentence about what a program is.
   • Support: single-choice “which is a program?”.  • Core: name a program for documents and one for the web.  • Challenge: explain why a song file is data, not a program. TA: “prompt, do not give the answer.”
4. (35–50) Unplugged — the intelligent piece of paper (mechanical noughts and crosses). Pupils follow the printed instructions like a machine; keep/screenshot the finished grid on the worksheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — pupils self-tick the 4 “I can…”. One sentence: “what is the difference between a computer and a calculator?”
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Under the hood — the hardware components
lessons.objectives:
I can name the main hardware components of a computer
I can describe what the processor, memory, storage, input/output and communication parts do
I can explain that all computers have the same basic parts (architecture)
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: hardware · processor (CPU) · memory (RAM) · storage · input · output · communication · architecture. TA: starter worksheet open; have a real device to point at. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — recap. Show the space-rover image; pupils CHOOSE the main job of any computer (run programs) on the starter worksheet. Link to last lesson.
   • Support: choose what ‘hardware’ means.  • Core: name two pieces of hardware in front of them.  • Challenge: a rover, a phone and a laptop look different — what do they all have inside?
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — the universal components. Teacher names processor, memory, storage, input/output, communication and what each does (I do), using the processor-chip image. Class does the MATCHING (component → what it does) on the activity worksheet (we do). Likely error: confuses memory and storage → fix-words “memory forgets when the power is off; storage remembers.”
3. (25–40) You-do — card-sort INPUT vs OUTPUT devices; then a level question.
   • Support: which part runs the instructions?  • Core: fill the ‘D is for Digital’ gap sentences (word bank given).  • Challenge: explain the difference between memory and storage. TA: “prompt with the word bank.”
4. (40–52) Label the device — label the parts on the Google Glass image (camera, display, touchpad, frame). Almost no writing; drag the labels.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”. One sentence: “every computer, big or small, has the same…?”
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks built in; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Orchestra conductor — how the parts work together
lessons.objectives:
I can describe how the processor, memory and storage work together to run a program
I can put the steps of running a program in the right order
I can say what an operating system is and what it does
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: processor · memory · storage · operating system · fetch · execute · load. TA: activity worksheet open; the two short system-monitor videos ready. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — ‘forbidden words’ recap. Pupils CHOOSE the processor’s job on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: storage keeps files even when the power is off?  • Core: one sentence on what main memory (RAM) does.  • Challenge: why is the processor like an ‘orchestra conductor’?
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — what happens when you open a program. Teacher walks the journey storage → memory → processor (I do) using the planetarium image. Class does the ORDER activity (drag the behind-the-scenes steps into order) on the activity worksheet (we do). Likely error: “the program runs straight from storage” → fix-words “it is COPIED into memory first.”
3. (25–40) You-do — watch the short system-monitor videos (Idle, Planetarium). Pupils PREDICT what happens to memory when a big program opens, then check it. Write the readings + screenshot in Show your work.
   • Support: what is an operating system?  • Core: why does the processor load go up?  • Challenge: how does ONE processor seem to run many programs at once? TA: pause the video on the readings.
4. (40–50) Operating system — define it as the program that controls the computer and shares the hardware.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”. “Describe what happens when you power up your computer.”
Differentiation on the SAME task. Videos have motion/sound — teacher-played, paused on the readings (teacher’s choice given the low-arousal default). No flashing on slides.

lessons.title: It's only logical — Boolean logic and logic gates
lessons.objectives:
I can describe the NOT, AND and OR logic operators
I can complete a truth table for NOT, AND and OR
I can explain that hardware is built from logic gates and uses 1s and 0s
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: logic · true · false · NOT · AND · OR · truth table · logic gate · binary (1s and 0s). TA: starter worksheet open; Turing Tumble gears video ready. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — gears as logic. Teacher plays the short Turing Tumble gears video (hook): a gear mechanism behaves like a NOT/AND gate. Pupils CHOOSE that a logic value can only be true or false on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: ‘NOT true’ is…?  • Core: fill the gap — AND/OR rules (word bank).  • Challenge: describe when ‘A AND (NOT B)’ is true.
2. (10–30) I-do → we-do — Boolean logic. Teacher models NOT, AND, OR with everyday examples and truth tables (I do). Class does the MATCHING (operator → what it means) on the activity worksheet (we do). Likely error: confuses OR with AND → fix-words “AND needs BOTH; OR needs at least one.”
3. (30–45) You-do — complete the truth tables (true/false tick questions, auto-marked). Build a simple AND-gate circuit in CircuitVerse; paste a link/screenshot in Show your work.
   • Support: NOT + an easy AND row.  • Core: AND/OR mix.  • Challenge: combined expressions. TA: read each row aloud before they pick.
4. (45–52) Big picture — hardware is built from logic gates; show the adder circuit image. Multi-select: what is true about logic gates. Plenary uses the Boole Google Doodle.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”. “Everything in a computer is made of switches that are…?” (on/off, 1/0).
Differentiation on the SAME task. Video has motion/sound — teacher-played hook, short (teacher’s choice). No flashing on slides.

lessons.title: Thinking machines — artificial intelligence and machine learning
lessons.objectives:
I can give a simple definition of artificial intelligence and machine learning
I can give real-world examples of AI and machine learning
I can describe the steps to train a machine: gather, train, test
I can say how machine learning is different from ordinary programming
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: artificial intelligence (AI) · machine learning (ML) · data · examples · train · test. TA: webcams and a set of objects (e.g. fruit) ready; starter worksheet open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — ‘words matter’. Pupils CHOOSE what ‘artificial’ means on the starter worksheet, then think of synonyms for ‘intelligent’.
   • Support: what does AI stand for?  • Core: two words meaning ‘intelligent’.  • Challenge: write your own one-sentence definition of AI.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — defining AI/ML; can a machine ‘think’? Teacher gives broad definitions and examples (I do); class decides which everyday things use AI (we do).
3. (20–45) You-do — Be the teacher (Google Teachable Machine). Pupils GATHER images, TRAIN, then TEST a fruit classifier (drag/click, almost no writing), using the Teachable Machine image as a guide. Put the ORDER activity (gather → train → test) on the activity worksheet first so they know the steps. Likely error: too few/poor examples → fix-words “more good examples = better guesses.”
   • Support: how many examples do you need?  • Core: why might the model get it wrong?  • Challenge: an AI decides who gets a loan — why do the training examples matter (fairness)?
4. (45–52) Card-sort — is this ordinary PROGRAMMING or MACHINE LEARNING? Plenary slider: how confident are you explaining ML?
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; one moral question about an AI use. Show their trained model.
Differentiation on the SAME task; calm role (operate the webcam / sort objects) for anyone who prefers it. No flashing/sound on slides.

resources.title: Google Teachable Machine — new project (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Sharing — recap and open source software
lessons.objectives:
I can recall the key ideas about computing systems from this unit
I can explain what it means to share and 'remix' program code
I can explain one benefit and one risk of open source software
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: hardware · software · open source · share · see inside · remix · attribution. TA: recap (summative) worksheet open; the ‘Naughty elf’ Scratch link ready. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–25) Recap quiz — the recap worksheet pulls together the whole unit: card-sort HARDWARE vs SOFTWARE, ‘which are programs?’ (multi-select), ‘what does a processor do?’ (multi-select). Low-stakes, mostly tick/drag. Anyone who finishes early starts the open-source activity.
   • Support: hardware/software card-sort + one-choice questions.  • Core/Challenge: the multi-selects + a short explain. TA: read options aloud.
2. (25–40) Implications — explore the ‘Naughty elf’ Scratch program; use ‘See inside’ to read the code (it is cheating!). Pupils CHOOSE what ‘See inside’ does on the activity worksheet; card-sort BENEFIT vs RISK of sharing code. Likely error: “remix changes the original” → fix-words “remix makes a COPY and credits you.”
3. (40–50) Free and open source software — define it (you can see, use and change the code). Pupils name one open source program for an everyday task.
   • Support: what does open source mean?  • Core: name an open source program.  • Challenge: why is ‘see inside’ good for safety/trust?
4. (50–55) Plenary — slider: how confident with the whole unit? Self-tick the 3 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.
